attributes.stem:
An otherwise well 47-year-old woman presents with a three-month history of aching in her right leg, her symptoms being worse at the end of the day and eased by elevation of that limb. The discomfort she experiences is beginning to interfere with her job as a shop floor manager and she does not like the unsightliness of the veins on her legs.

On examination, there are obvious superficial varicosities and digital pressure applied to the saphenofemoral junction controls back-filling into these veins. Ultrasound scanning confirms that there is venous incompetence of the sapheno-femoral junction and great (long) saphenous vein. She has not found any symptomatic relief with support stockings.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following management options is the most appropriate management?
options: Endovenous ablation of the great saphenous vein
Ligation and stripping of the long saphenous vein
attributes.explanation:
Varicose veins are a manifestation of chronic venous insufficiency. They are elongated, tortuous, subcutaneous veins >3mm in diameter. The physical appearance of these varicosities is the most common concern to patients.

Prior to treatment of varicose veins, it is important to assess on Duplex ultrasound, the superficial and deep venous systems for evidence of reflux or obstruction.

Conservative approaches to chronic venous insufficiency include leg elevation, exercise and compression therapy to improve oxygen transport to the skin, as well as any skincare for stasis dermatitis.

If symptoms and signs persist despite conservative therapy, then venous ablation may be considered. Endovenous ablation may be thermal or non-thermal and has been shown to provide an overall benefit and similar long-term outcomes compared with the other, more invasive treatment options. Studies have shown that endovenous ablation (with laser or radiofrequency) is as effective as stripping in the short and medium-term. Endovenous techniques are less invasive and thus patients make a quicker recovery than from open surgery.

Contraindications to lower extremity endovenous ablation include acute deep vein thrombosis, superficial thrombophlebitis and pregnancy - due to an increased risk of thrombosis; as well as patients with moderate to severe peripheral arterial disease due to the increased risk for nonhealing ulcers. There should also be caution using endovenous ablation in patients with deep venous insufficiency due to the recurrence of varicosities following intervention.

Whilst support stockings might provide symptomatic relief from the aching, this approach will do little to the appearance of the varicosities. Whilst foam sclerotherapy is an accepted form of treatment for superficial varicosities, the long-term benefits are inferior to those achieved by laser or radiofrequency endogenous ablation. Ligation and stripping of the long saphenous vein and ligation of the saphenofemoral junction used to be the standard of care for primary varicose veins, however, this procedure has now been superseded by other, less invasive techniques. In addition, patients with significant peripheral artery disease may need their long saphenous vein for reconstructive bypass surgery, so a vascular assessment would be appropriate if arterial occlusive disease suspected on history or clinical examination.
learningPoints: Endovenous ablation is the mainstay of treatment for superficial varicosities.
references: Carroll C, Hummel S, Leaviss J, Ren S, Stevens JW, Cantrell A, et al. Systematic review, network meta-analysis and exploratory cost-effectiveness model of randomized trials of minimally invasive techniques versus surgery for varicose veins. Br J Surg. 2014 Aug;101(9):1040-52.

Nesbitt C, Bedenis R, Bhattacharya V, Stansby G. Endovenous ablation (radiofrequency and laser) and foam sclerotherapy versus open surgery for great saphenous vein varices. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014 Jul 30(7):Cd005624.
van den Bos R, Arends L, Kockaert M, Neumann M, Nijsten T. Endovenous therapies of lower extremity varicosities: a meta-analysis. J Vasc Surg. 2009 Jan;49(1):230-9.
Raetz J, Wilson M, Collins K. Varicose Veins: Diagnosis and Treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2019; 99:682-88.
